intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A natural-language interface to a single relational database, intended to answer read-only analytical questions for internal data consumers by translating questions into SQL, executing them, and summarizing the results. The agent may use `sql_db_list_tables`, `sql_db_schema`, `sql_db_query_checker`, and `sql_db_query` against a pre-configured SQLAlchemy connection, and its only output is a direct natural-language answer in the current session. Data- and schema-modifying statements (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, DROP/ALTER/TRUNCATE, GRANT/REVOKE, admin and multi-statement commands) are forbidden outright with no approval path, queries must be double-checked and row-limited, and the agent must operate within a bounded iteration cap. The remit further expects defense-in-depth — a read-only least-privilege DB role and server-side statement timeouts — and states explicitly that the agent's own logic MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point for the write prohibition.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: The classic `create_sql_agent` factory in `agent_toolkits/sql/base.py` builds a `langchain_classic` `AgentExecutor` over four tools from `SQLDatabaseToolkit` (`toolkit.py`), with prompt text in `agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py` and the query-checker prompt in `tools/sql_database/prompt.py`. The query tool `QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run` (`tools/sql_database/tool.py`) calls `SQLDatabase.run_no_throw`, which flows through `run` to `_execute` in `utilities/sql_database.py`, where any LLM-produced string is wrapped in `text()` and executed inside an `engine.begin()` committing transaction — no SQL parsing, denylist, statement-type check, or read-only enforcement exists anywhere in the scoped code. Real safeguards present in code are a default `max_iterations=15` cap, a `top_k=10` prompt-level row hint, and `max_string_length=300` result truncation via `truncate_word`. The maintainers attach an explicit `!!! warning` admonition in the `create_sql_agent` docstring stating the agent can execute arbitrary SQL (including unintended writes depending on DB permissions), flag `AgentExecutor` as legacy, and direct production users to apply least-privilege roles and server-side controls.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is policy-declared-in-prompt, zero code-level enforcement: every prohibition the remit treats as a hard MUST NOT — no DML, no DDL, no admin commands, no multi-statement — exists only as the prose line `DO NOT make any DML statements` in `agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py`, while the execution path `QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run` -> `run_no_throw` -> `_execute` wraps any LLM-generated string in `text()` and runs it inside a committing `engine.begin()` transaction with no statement-type gate. The remit's own clause that "the agent's own logic MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point" is therefore violated by construction — the LLM prompt is the only barrier in the code Praxen can see. This is a documented, maintainer-acknowledged design: the `create_sql_agent` docstring warns the agent runs arbitrary SQL and pushes the real controls (read-only roles, server-side timeouts) to the operator, so the divergence is between the library's in-code posture and the safe deployment the remit assumes. Genuine in-code mitigations — the `max_iterations=15` loop cap and `max_string_length` result truncation — are real and earn credit, but they bound runaway loops and output size, not the write prohibition.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every answer MUST be read-only and sourced from the database.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Result sets MUST be limited to a small default row count unless the user explicitly asks for more.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST query only the columns relevant to the user's question and MUST NOT select all columns of a table indiscriminately.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every generated SQL query MUST be double-checked before it is executed.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: If a question cannot be answered from the database, the agent MUST say so plainly rather than speculate.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST operate within a bounded number of tool-call iterations so that it cannot loop indefinitely.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute any data-modifying statement — inserts, updates, deletes, merges, upserts, replacements, or anything else that changes row data.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute any schema-, permission-, or structure-modifying statement — creating, dropping, altering, truncating, renaming, granting, or revoking.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute administrative or superuser commands, including bulk loads, attach/detach operations, and maintenance commands.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could conceal hidden data- or schema-modifying statements.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute queries that reach tables outside the scope it has been configured for.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT allow user-provided text to override its instructions, its role, or its prohibition on modifying the database.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT continue issuing tool calls past its iteration cap.

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Defense-in-depth expectations
rules.rule_text: The database connection SHOULD use a read-only role that cannot perform data- or schema-modifying statements even if the agent generates one.

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Defense-in-depth expectations
rules.rule_text: Statement timeouts SHOULD be enforced at the database or connection layer.

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Defense-in-depth expectations
rules.rule_text: The agent's own logic MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point for the prohibition on modifying the database.

findings.summary: The no-DML / no-DDL / no-admin prohibition exists only as an LLM prompt line; the query tool executes any SQL the model emits with no code-level gate.
findings.description: The remit forbids INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, DROP/ALTER/TRUNCATE/GRANT/REVOKE, and admin commands outright, but the only enforcement is the prose line `DO NOT make any DML statements` in `agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py`. `QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run` passes the LLM string to `run_no_throw` -> `_execute`, which runs `text(command)` inside a committing `engine.begin()` transaction with no statement-type check. Any jailbreak or injected instruction that produces a write or schema statement executes and commits.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every answer MUST be read-only and sourced from the database. / The agent MUST NOT execute any data-modifying statement — inserts, updates, deletes, merges, upserts, replacements, or anything else that changes row data. / The agent MUST NOT execute any schema-, permission-, or structure-modifying statement — creating, dropping, altering, truncating, renaming, granting, or revoking. / The agent MUST NOT execute administrative or superuser commands, including bulk loads, attach/detach operations, and maintenance commands.

evidence.snippet: "DO NOT make any DML statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP etc.) — the sole prohibition, in prompt text only, no code counterpart"
"_execute wraps command in text() and runs it inside engine.begin() (committing) with no statement-type or read-only guard; reached via QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run -> run_no_throw"

recommended_actions: Do not rely on the prompt: enforce the write prohibition outside the agent by connecting `SQLDatabase` with a least-privilege read-only DB role, as the `create_sql_agent` docstring already advises.
If in-process defense is wanted, add a statement-type gate (parse/inspect the command in `_execute` or a `QuerySQLDatabaseTool` wrapper) that rejects anything but read statements before execution.

findings.summary: Query results and schema rows flow into LLM context unlabeled, giving injected row/table-name content a one-hop path to drive arbitrary SQL through the ungated query tool.
findings.description: The remit forbids following instructions embedded in query results, table names, or row contents, but `run` and `get_table_info` feed result cells and sample rows straight into the model with only length truncation and no trust labeling. Because the query tool has no statement-type gate (see PRAX-2026-06-15-001), an indirect injection carried in attacker-controlled table data can redirect the agent to emit a DML/DDL statement that then executes. This is the compound external-content-to-execution chain, not two independent gaps.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT allow user-provided text to override its instructions, its role, or its prohibition on modifying the database. / The agent MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents.

evidence.snippet: "run() returns truncated-but-raw result cells into LLM context with no untrusted-content labeling"
"_get_sample_rows pulls raw row values into schema text fed to the model via get_table_info / InfoSQLDatabaseTool"

recommended_actions: Treat all DB-returned content (rows, table/column names, sample rows) as untrusted in prompt construction and pair it with the read-only role from PRAX-2026-06-15-001 so an injected write cannot execute.

findings.summary: The remit's "agent logic MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point" for the write prohibition is violated by construction — the LLM prompt is the only barrier in the scoped code.
findings.description: The remit explicitly requires that the agent's own logic not be the sole enforcement point for the prohibition on modifying the database, anticipating server-side defense-in-depth. Within the scoped code there is no deterministic enforcement layer of any kind — no read-only role assertion, no statement parser, no execution-option restriction — so the LLM's adherence to a prompt line is in fact the only thing standing between a generated write and a committed one. The maintainer docstring acknowledges this and pushes the real control to the operator, but as shipped the library satisfies the clause only if the deployer adds the external control.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent's own logic MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point for the prohibition on modifying the database.

evidence.snippet: "_execute is the single execution chokepoint and applies no read-only / statement-type enforcement before connection.execute"
"QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run delegates straight to db.run_no_throw with no pre-execution policy check"

recommended_actions: Make a non-LLM layer authoritative for the write prohibition — a read-only DB role is the lowest-effort durable control; document it as required (not optional) for any deployment that exposes untrusted questions.

findings.summary: No code-level block on multi-statement or stored-procedure queries; the prohibition relies on the prompt and the DBAPI driver's incidental behavior.
findings.description: The remit forbids multi-statement queries and stored-procedure invocations that could conceal hidden write statements, but `_execute` passes the LLM string to `text()` and executes it without splitting, inspecting, or rejecting compound statements. Whether a stacked statement is refused depends entirely on the underlying DBAPI driver, not on any agent control, and stored-procedure calls (which can perform writes) are not addressed at all.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could conceal hidden data- or schema-modifying statements.

evidence.snippet: "command wrapped in text() and executed as-is; no statement-count or stored-procedure inspection before connection.execute"

recommended_actions: Add multi-statement/stored-procedure rejection in the query path (or rely on a read-only role that cannot invoke writing procedures), and do not treat driver-incidental single-statement behavior as a control.

findings.summary: The default row-count limit is a prompt-level `top_k` hint, not a code-enforced cap; only per-cell string length is bounded in code.
findings.description: The remit requires result sets be limited to a small default row count, but `top_k=10` is injected into the prompt as guidance ("limit your query to at most {top_k} results") rather than enforced. `run` bounds each cell via `max_string_length` truncation but applies no row-count ceiling, so an LLM that omits or miscomputes a LIMIT returns the full result set.
findings.policy_rule_text: Result sets MUST be limited to a small default row count unless the user explicitly asks for more.

evidence.snippet: "row limit expressed as prompt instruction 'always limit your query to at most {top_k} results' — no code enforcement"
"run truncates cell strings via truncate_word but applies no row-count cap to the result list"

recommended_actions: Enforce a hard server-side or wrapper-level row ceiling (e.g. apply a fetch limit in `_execute`) rather than relying on the model to honor `top_k`.

findings.summary: The "double-check every query" requirement is satisfied by an advisory LLM checker tool whose use is prompt-encouraged but not enforced before execution.
findings.description: The remit requires every generated query be double-checked before execution. `QuerySQLCheckerTool` exists and the toolkit description says "Always use this tool before executing," but nothing in code sequences the checker ahead of `sql_db_query` — the agent can call the query tool directly. The check is also itself an LLM pass over the query, so it validates syntax/common-mistakes, not the write prohibition.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated SQL query MUST be double-checked before it is executed.

evidence.snippet: "QuerySQLCheckerTool is a separate optional tool; ordering before sql_db_query is requested in prompt/description only, not enforced"
"checker description 'Always use this tool before executing' — advisory text, no execution-order gate"

recommended_actions: If double-check is a hard requirement, gate `QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run` so it refuses to execute a query that has not passed the checker, rather than relying on the prompt.

findings.summary: No logging or audit of SQL execution exists in the scoped code, leaving DML, out-of-scope access, or injection-driven queries undetectable from this layer.
findings.description: The remit's escalation expectations and the Monitor Continuously category both assume the agent's actions can be observed, but `_execute`, `run`, and `run_no_throw` emit no log record of the statement executed, the caller, or the result. With no structured action trail, a query that violates the write prohibition or reaches an out-of-scope table leaves no evidence at the agent layer.

evidence.snippet: "_execute runs the query with no logging/audit emission of statement, caller, or result"
"QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run executes via run_no_throw with no per-call logging"

recommended_actions: Add structured, statement-level logging at the `_execute` chokepoint (query text, dialect, row count, outcome) so DML and out-of-scope access are detectable, and forward to the deployment's audit sink.

findings.summary: Table-scope restriction is a Python-level name filter; the query tool still executes raw SQL, so a statement naming any role-visible table runs regardless of `include_tables`.
findings.description: The remit forbids reaching tables outside the configured scope. `include_tables`/`ignore_tables` filter the names surfaced by `get_usable_table_names` and reflected metadata, but they do not constrain `sql_db_query` — `_execute` runs whatever table references the LLM writes, so a query naming an excluded table that the connection's DB role can see will execute. The name filter shapes discovery, not authorization.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute queries that reach tables outside the scope it has been configured for.

evidence.snippet: "get_usable_table_names applies include_tables/ignore_tables to listing only; not consulted by the query execution path"
"_execute runs arbitrary table references in the LLM query with no allowlist check against _include_tables"

recommended_actions: Enforce table scope at the DB role/schema-grant layer (the authoritative control) rather than via the Python name filter, which only governs what the agent is told about.

findings.summary: Per-query execution-time limiting is a client-side `max_execution_time` on the AgentExecutor, not a DB statement timeout; the maintainers note client timeouts may not cancel a running statement.
findings.description: The remit expects per-query execution-time limits where the environment allows. `create_sql_agent` plumbs `max_execution_time` into the `AgentExecutor`, but this bounds the agent's overall wall-clock loop, not an individual database statement, and the docstring itself warns that client-side timeouts do not always cancel a running server statement. No statement-level timeout is set at the connection or engine layer in the scoped code.
findings.policy_rule_text: Statement timeouts SHOULD be enforced at the database or connection layer.

evidence.snippet: "max_execution_time passed to AgentExecutor — agent-loop wall clock, not a DB statement timeout"
"docstring: 'Client-side timeouts do not always guarantee that a running statement is cancelled on the database server'"

recommended_actions: Set statement timeouts at the connection/engine layer (e.g. via `engine_args` / driver options or `execution_options`) so a long-running query is cancelled server-side, per the maintainer guidance.

positives.title: Bounded tool-call iterations cap runaway loops
positives.description: `create_sql_agent` sets `max_iterations=15` by default and passes it (with `early_stopping_method`) into the `AgentExecutor`, giving a real, code-level bound on the agent's tool-call loop that satisfies the remit's iteration-cap requirement.

positives.title: Query result truncation limits output size
positives.description: `SQLDatabase.run` truncates every returned cell through `truncate_word` against `max_string_length` (default 300), a genuine in-code bound on how much data a single query can surface into the LLM context and the response.

positives.title: Explicit maintainer security warning and least-privilege guidance
positives.description: The `create_sql_agent` docstring carries a prominent `!!! warning` that the agent executes arbitrary SQL (including unintended writes depending on DB permissions), flags `AgentExecutor` as legacy, and directs production users to least-privilege read-only roles and server-side statement timeouts — the correct defense-in-depth framing.

log_files.no_logs_note: No logging or audit infrastructure exists in the scoped code; SQL execution in `_execute` emits no record of the statement, caller, or result, which is itself the basis of the Monitor Continuously gap (see PRAX-2026-06-15-007).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The agent inherits a coherent, narrow shape from the toolkit (four SQL tools, a single mission) and ships real loop and output-size bounds, but the controls that matter most for its declared intent — the read-only / no-DML prohibition — have no code-level enforcement at all and live entirely in an LLM prompt that injected row content can target. Zero Trust is the floor: the LLM is the sole interposition on a tool that executes arbitrary SQL in a committing transaction. Supply-chain, red-team, and monitoring posture are essentially unaddressed within the scoped code, though the maintainers' explicit security warning and least-privilege guidance keep this from being Absent.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The toolkit fixes a narrow four-tool SQL surface and the prompt scopes the agent to database Q&A with an "I don't know" fallback, but domain enforcement is prompt-only — `sql_db_query` will execute any SQL the LLM emits, including against tables outside the configured `include_tables` set, with no code gate.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: `run` truncates returned cells via `max_string_length` and the toolkit scopes context to schema/sample rows, a real if partial bound on what enters the model, but that content is fed in unlabeled with no untrusted-source marking, so attacker-controlled table/column names and row values can carry instructions the agent cannot distinguish from operator intent.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: There is no code-level interposition on the agent's SQL execution — `QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run` passes the LLM string to `run_no_throw` -> `_execute`, which runs `text(command)` inside a committing `engine.begin()` with no statement-type check, denylist, or read-only guard; the only stated control is an LLM prompt line, and the remit's "MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point" clause is violated by construction.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The scoped code is a maintained part of `langchain-community` with no credentials in-tree, but the scope carries no dependency pinning, SBOM, or model-version posture of its own, and the query-checker silently depends on a `langchain_classic` LLMChain import resolved at runtime.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artifacts are present in the scoped paths; the maintainers' explicit security warning shows awareness of the arbitrary-SQL risk but there is no evidence within scope that injection or DML-bypass testing drove the design.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The scoped code emits no logging, audit, or structured event on tool calls or SQL execution — `_execute` runs queries with no record of statement, caller, or result — leaving DML or out-of-scope access undetectable at this layer; the maintainer docstring's monitor/alert-on-slow-queries guidance is operator-side only and unimplemented in code.
